question:
Let
\[
\vec{G}(x,y) = \left( \frac{-y}{x^2+4y^2}, \frac{x}{x^2+4y^2},0
\right).
\]
Prove or disprove that there is a vector-valued function
\[
\vec{F}(x,y,z) = (M(x,y,z), N(x,y,z), P(x,y,z))
\]
with the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $M,N,P$ have continuous partial derivatives for all
$(x,y,z) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(ii)] $\mathrm{Curl}\,\vec{F} = \vec{0}$ for all $(x,y,z) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(iii)] $\vec{F}(x,y,0) = \vec{G}(x,y)$.
\end{enumerate}
solution:
Solution. Let \( S \) be a surface not containing \( (0,0,0) \) whose boundary \( \partial S \) is the ellipse \( x^{2}+4 y^{2}-4=z=0 \) parameterized by \( (2 \cos \theta, \sin \theta, 0) \) for \( 0 \leq \theta \leq 2 \pi \). (For instance, \( S \) could be the half of the ellipsoid \( x^{2}+4 y^{2}+z^{2}=4 \) with \( z \geq 0 \).) If \( F \) exists, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\iint_{S}(\operatorname{Curl} \vec{F}) \cdot \vec{n} d S \quad(\text { since Curl } \vec{F}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { on } S) \\
& =\int_{\partial S} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r} \quad(\text { by Stokes' Theorem, e.g. [Ap2, Theorem 12.3]) } \\
& =\int_{\partial S} \vec{G} \cdot d \vec{r} \quad(\text { since } \vec{F}=\vec{G} \text { on } \partial S) \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(\frac{-\sin \theta}{4}, \frac{2 \cos \theta}{4}, 0\right) \cdot(-2 \sin \theta, \cos \theta, 0) d \theta \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{1}{2} d \theta \\
& =\pi
\end{aligned}
\]
a contradiction.

descriptive_long.question:
Let
\[
\vec{vectorg}(xcoord,ycoord) = \left( \frac{-ycoord}{xcoord^2+4ycoord^2}, \frac{xcoord}{xcoord^2+4ycoord^2},0
\right).
\]
Prove or disprove that there is a vector-valued function
\[
\vec{vectorf}(xcoord,ycoord,zcoord) = (funcvalm(xcoord,ycoord,zcoord), funcvaln(xcoord,ycoord,zcoord), funcvalp(xcoord,ycoord,zcoord))
\]
with the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $funcvalm,funcvaln,funcvalp$ have continuous partial derivatives for all
$(xcoord,ycoord,zcoord) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(ii)] $\mathrm{Curl}\,\vec{vectorf} = \vec{0}$ for all $(xcoord,ycoord,zcoord) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(iii)] $\vec{vectorf}(xcoord,ycoord,0) = \vec{vectorg}(xcoord,ycoord)$.
\end{enumerate}
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( surfaces \) be a surface not containing \( (0,0,0) \) whose boundary \( \partial surfaces \) is the ellipse \( xcoord^{2}+4 ycoord^{2}-4=zcoord=0 \) parameterized by \( (2 \cos angleth, \sin angleth, 0) \) for \( 0 \leq angleth \leq 2 \pi \). (For instance, \( surfaces \) could be the half of the ellipsoid \( xcoord^{2}+4 ycoord^{2}+zcoord^{2}=4 \) with \( zcoord \geq 0 \).) If \( vectorf \) exists, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\iint_{surfaces}(\operatorname{Curl} \vec{vectorf}) \cdot \vec{normalv} d surfaces \quad(\text { since Curl } \vec{vectorf}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { on } surfaces) \\
& =\int_{\partial surfaces} \vec{vectorf} \cdot d \vec{pathvec} \quad(\text { by Stokes' Theorem, e.g. [Ap2, Theorem 12.3]) } \\
& =\int_{\partial surfaces} \vec{vectorg} \cdot d \vec{pathvec} \quad(\text { since } \vec{vectorf}=\vec{vectorg} \text { on } \partial surfaces) \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(\frac{-\sin angleth}{4}, \frac{2 \cos angleth}{4}, 0\right) \cdot(-2 \sin angleth, \cos angleth, 0) d angleth \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{1}{2} d angleth \\
& =\pi
\end{aligned}
\]
a contradiction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let
\[
\vec{snowflake}(lighthouse,crosswind) = \left( \frac{-crosswind}{lighthouse^{2}+4crosswind^{2}}, \frac{lighthouse}{lighthouse^{2}+4crosswind^{2}},0
\right).
\]
Prove or disprove that there is a vector-valued function
\[
\vec{kingfisher}(lighthouse,crosswind,drumstick) = (turnpike(lighthouse,crosswind,drumstick), cloudburst(lighthouse,crosswind,drumstick), dreamcatch(lighthouse,crosswind,drumstick))
\]
with the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $turnpike,cloudburst,dreamcatch$ have continuous partial derivatives for all $(lighthouse,crosswind,drumstick) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(ii)] $\mathrm{Curl}\,\vec{kingfisher} = \vec{0}$ for all $(lighthouse,crosswind,drumstick) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(iii)] $\vec{kingfisher}(lighthouse,crosswind,0) = \vec{snowflake}(lighthouse,crosswind)$.
\end{enumerate}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( driftwood \) be a surface not containing \( (0,0,0) \) whose boundary \( \partial driftwood \) is the ellipse \( lighthouse^{2}+4crosswind^{2}-4=drumstick=0 \) parameterized by \( (2 \cos raincloud, \sin raincloud, 0) \) for \( 0 \leq raincloud \leq 2 \pi \). (For instance, \( driftwood \) could be the half of the ellipsoid \( lighthouse^{2}+4crosswind^{2}+drumstick^{2}=4 \) with \( drumstick \geq 0 \).) If \( kingfisher \) exists, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\iint_{driftwood}(\operatorname{Curl} \vec{kingfisher}) \cdot \vec{sandpaper} d driftwood \quad(\text { since Curl } \vec{kingfisher}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { on } driftwood) \\
& =\int_{\partial driftwood} \vec{kingfisher} \cdot d \vec{starfruit} \quad(\text { by Stokes' Theorem, e.g. [Ap2, Theorem 12.3]) } \\
& =\int_{\partial driftwood} \vec{snowflake} \cdot d \vec{starfruit} \quad(\text { since } \vec{kingfisher}=\vec{snowflake} \text { on } \partial driftwood) \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(\frac{-\sin raincloud}{4}, \frac{2 \cos raincloud}{4}, 0\right) \cdot(-2 \sin raincloud, \cos raincloud, 0) d raincloud \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{1}{2} d raincloud \\
& =\pi
\end{aligned}
\]
a contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let
\[
\vec{unknownfield}(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis) = \left( \frac{-horizontalaxis}{verticalaxis^{2}+4horizontalaxis^{2}}, \frac{verticalaxis}{verticalaxis^{2}+4horizontalaxis^{2}},0
\right).
\]
Prove or disprove that there is a vector-valued function
\[
\vec{destroyer}(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,surfaceaxis) = (combinedfun(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,surfaceaxis), singularfun(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,surfaceaxis), voidfunct(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,surfaceaxis))
\]
with the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $combinedfun,singularfun,voidfunct$ have continuous partial derivatives for all
$(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,surfaceaxis) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(ii)] $\mathrm{Curl}\,\vec{destroyer} = \vec{0}$ for all $(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,surfaceaxis) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(iii)] $\vec{destroyer}(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis,0) = \vec{unknownfield}(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis)$.
\end{enumerate}
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( volumeobj \) be a surface not containing \( (0,0,0) \) whose boundary \( \partial volumeobj \) is the ellipse \( verticalaxis^{2}+4 horizontalaxis^{2}-4=surfaceaxis=0 \) parameterized by \( (2 \cos straightedge, \sin straightedge, 0) \) for \( 0 \le straightedge \le 2 \pi \). (For instance, \( volumeobj \) could be the half of the ellipsoid \( verticalaxis^{2}+4 horizontalaxis^{2}+surfaceaxis^{2}=4 \) with \( surfaceaxis \ge 0 \).) If \( destroyer \) exists, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\iint_{volumeobj}(\operatorname{Curl} \vec{destroyer}) \cdot \vec{tangentvec} d volumeobj \quad(\text { since Curl } \vec{destroyer}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { on } volumeobj) \\
& =\int_{\partial volumeobj} \vec{destroyer} \cdot d \vec{anchorpoint} \quad(\text { by Stokes' Theorem, e.g. [Ap2, Theorem 12.3]) } \\
& =\int_{\partial volumeobj} \vec{unknownfield} \cdot d \vec{anchorpoint} \quad(\text { since } \vec{destroyer}=\vec{unknownfield} \text { on } \partial volumeobj) \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(\frac{-\sin straightedge}{4}, \frac{2 \cos straightedge}{4}, 0\right) \cdot(-2 \sin straightedge, \cos straightedge, 0) d straightedge \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{1}{2} d straightedge \\
& =\pi
\end{aligned}
\]
a contradiction.

garbled_string.question:
Let
\[
\vec{czlqevsn}(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) = \left( \frac{-hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp^2+4hjgrksla^2}, \frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^2+4hjgrksla^2},0
\right).
\]
Prove or disprove that there is a vector-valued function
\[
\vec{kjdpqraw}(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mndpybxe) = (rtfvknaj(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mndpybxe), wqkpsdml(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mndpybxe), vnrybega(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mndpybxe))
\]
with the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $rtfvknaj,wqkpsdml,vnrybega$ have continuous partial derivatives for all
$(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mndpybxe) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(ii)] $\mathrm{Curl}\,\vec{kjdpqraw} = \vec{0}$ for all $(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mndpybxe) \neq (0,0,0)$;
\item[(iii)] $\vec{kjdpqraw}(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,0) = \vec{czlqevsn}(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)$.
\end{enumerate}
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( xeflmpdo \) be a surface not containing \( (0,0,0) \) whose boundary \( \partial xeflmpdo \) is the ellipse \( qzxwvtnp^{2}+4 hjgrksla^{2}-4=mndpybxe=0 \) parameterized by \( (2 \cos fljsgkra, \sin fljsgkra, 0) \) for \( 0 \leq fljsgkra \leq 2 \pi \). (For instance, \( xeflmpdo \) could be the half of the ellipsoid \( qzxwvtnp^{2}+4 hjgrksla^{2}+mndpybxe^{2}=4 \) with \( mndpybxe \geq 0 \).) If \( kjdpqraw \) exists, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =\iint_{xeflmpdo}(\operatorname{Curl} \vec{kjdpqraw}) \cdot \vec{dbchoyva} d xeflmpdo \quad(\text { since Curl } \vec{kjdpqraw}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { on } xeflmpdo) \\
& =\int_{\partial xeflmpdo} \vec{kjdpqraw} \cdot d \vec{kpmtosle} \quad(\text { by Stokes' Theorem, e.g. [Ap2, Theorem 12.3]) } \\
& =\int_{\partial xeflmpdo} \vec{czlqevsn} \cdot d \vec{kpmtosle} \quad(\text { since } \vec{kjdpqraw}=\vec{czlqevsn} \text { on } \partial xeflmpdo) \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(\frac{-\sin fljsgkra}{4}, \frac{2 \cos fljsgkra}{4}, 0\right) \cdot(-2 \sin fljsgkra, \cos fljsgkra, 0) d fljsgkra \\
& =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{1}{2} d fljsgkra \\
& =\pi
\end{aligned}
\]
a contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let the following three C^1-vector fields be prescribed on the punctured coordinate planes of \mathbb{R}^3:

G_1(x,y) = ( -x^2y /(x^2 + y^2)^2 ,  x y^2 /(x^2 + y^2)^2 , 0 )    for (x,y) \neq  (0,0) and z = 0  
G_2(y,z) = ( 0 , -y^2z /(y^2 + z^2)^2 ,  y z^2 /(y^2 + z^2)^2 )    for (y,z) \neq  (0,0) and x = 0  
G_3(z,x) = ( -z x^2 /(z^2 + x^2)^2 , 0 ,  x z^2 /(z^2 + x^2)^2 )    for (z,x) \neq  (0,0) and y = 0.

Observe that each field vanishes when one of its two variables is zero, so on every coordinate axis  
 (0,y,0), (x,0,0), (0,0,z)  
all three prescriptions give the common value (0,0,0); the data are therefore mutually compatible along the pairwise intersections of the planes.

Question.  Does there exist a C^1-vector field  
  F : \mathbb{R}^3 \ {(0,0,0)} \to  \mathbb{R}^3  (F = (M,N,P))  
satisfying simultaneously

(i) curl F \equiv  0 on \mathbb{R}^3 \ {(0,0,0)};  

(ii) F coincides with the given traces on the three coordinate planes, i.e.  
   F(x,y,0)=G_1(x,y), F(0,y,z)=G_2(y,z), F(x,0,z)=G_3(z,x);  

(iii) |F(r)| = O(|r|^{-1}) as |r| \to  \infty  ?

Either construct such a field or prove that none can exist.
kernel_variant.solution:
We show that conditions (i)-(iii) are incompatible; no such vector field F exists.

1.  Topological background  
The domain \mathbb{R}^3\{0} is simply connected.  Hence any C^1-vector field whose curl is identically zero there is conservative: for every piece-wise smooth closed curve C contained in \mathbb{R}^3\{0},
  \oint _C F\cdot dr = 0.                                              (1)

2.  A closed curve touching all three planes  
Join three quarter-circles of radius 1, one in each coordinate plane, head-to-tail:

C_1 : \theta \in [0,\pi /2] \to  (cos \theta , sin \theta , 0)   (XY-plane)  
C_2 : \theta \in [0,\pi /2] \to  (0, cos \theta , sin \theta )   (YZ-plane)  
C_3 : \theta \in [0,\pi /2] \to  (sin \theta , 0, cos \theta )   (ZX-plane)

Let C = C_1 \cup  C_2 \cup  C_3.  C is a closed, piece-wise C^1 loop lying in \mathbb{R}^3\{0}; it meets each coordinate plane exactly once and avoids the coordinate axes because every component is non-negative and at least one of them is strictly positive along each segment.

Because of (1),
  \oint _C F\cdot dr = 0.                                              (2)

On each quarter-arc the integrand equals the prescribed trace (condition (ii)); we can therefore compute the three contributions explicitly.

3.  Integral over C_1 (XY-plane)  
Parameterisation: r(\theta ) = (cos \theta , sin \theta , 0), r'(\theta ) = (-sin \theta , cos \theta , 0).

G_1(r(\theta )) = ( -cos^2\theta  sin \theta  , cos \theta  sin^2\theta  , 0 )          (since (cos^2\theta +sin^2\theta )^2=1).

Dot product:  
G_1\cdot r' = (-cos^2\theta  sin \theta )(-sin \theta ) + (cos \theta  sin^2\theta )(cos \theta )  
    = 2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta .

Hence  
I_1 := \int _{C_1} F\cdot dr = \int _0^{\pi /2} 2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta  d\theta   
   = \int _0^{\pi /2} (1/2) sin^2(2\theta ) d\theta   
   = (1/4)\int _0^{\pi } sin^2u du  (u = 2\theta )  
   = (1/4)\cdot (\pi /2) = \pi /8.                               (3)

4.  Integral over C_2 (YZ-plane)  
Parameterisation: r(\theta ) = (0, cos \theta , sin \theta ), r'(\theta ) = (0, -sin \theta , cos \theta ).

G_2(r(\theta )) = ( 0, -cos^2\theta  sin \theta  , cos \theta  sin^2\theta  ).

Dot product: G_2\cdot r' = (-cos^2\theta  sin \theta )(-sin \theta ) + (cos \theta  sin^2\theta )(cos \theta )  
       = 2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta .

Therefore  
I_2 := \int _{C_2} F\cdot dr = \pi /8.                               (4)

5.  Integral over C_3 (ZX-plane)  
Parameterisation: r(\theta ) = (sin \theta , 0, cos \theta ), r'(\theta ) = (cos \theta , 0, -sin \theta ).

G_3(r(\theta )) = ( -cos \theta  sin^2\theta  , 0 , sin \theta  cos^2\theta  ).

Dot product: G_3\cdot r' = (-cos \theta  sin^2\theta )(cos \theta ) + (sin \theta  cos^2\theta )(-sin \theta )  
       = -2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta .

Thus  
I_3 := \int _{C_3} F\cdot dr = -\pi /8.                              (5)

6.  Total circulation around C  
Because F coincides with the three traces on the corresponding segments,

\oint _C F\cdot dr = I_1 + I_2 + I_3  
     = \pi /8 + \pi /8 - \pi /8 = \pi /8.                       (6)

7.  Contradiction  
Equations (2) and (6) are incompatible: (2) asserts the circulation is 0, whereas (6) shows it equals \pi /8.  This contradiction proves that a C^1 vector field satisfying (i)-(iii) cannot exist.

8.  Remarks  
*  Compatibility of the data along the coordinate axes (all three traces are identically zero there) removes the elementary ``two different values at one point'' obstruction, forcing one to use a genuine Stokes/circulation argument.  
*  The decay assumption (iii) is satisfied by each trace (|G_i(r)| \lesssim  |r|^{-1}) and rules out pathological compensating fields at infinity; it does not alter the contradiction obtained above.  
*  The loop C spans a surface that avoids the origin, so Stokes' theorem may legitimately be applied in both directions of the argument.

Hence no C^1-vector field F with properties (i)-(iii) exists.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimensional constraints: the unknown field must now satisfy three independent boundary conditions—one on each coordinate plane—rather than on a single plane.  
• Multiple interacting concepts: the argument must keep track of how these separate restrictions interact on a composite spatial path occupying all three planes.  
• More calculations: three separate (and unequal) rational integrals are required; each demands a change of variables and careful trigonometric/algebraic manipulation.  
• Deeper theory: understanding why a single path integral must split into pieces but still cancel necessitates a clear grasp of Stokes’ theorem on a piecewise-smooth surface, homology of punctured space, and how line-integral invariants behave under piecewise definitions.  
• Logical subtlety: none of the three planar data sets alone blocks the existence of F (each can be realised by a standard Aharonov–Bohm potential); the impossibility emerges only when all three are imposed simultaneously, so the solver must design a path that samples every restriction at once.

All of this represents a significant escalation in technical detail, length of argument, and conceptual depth compared with the original single-plane variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let the following three C^1-vector fields be prescribed on the punctured coordinate planes of \mathbb{R}^3:

G_1(x,y) = ( -x^2y /(x^2 + y^2)^2 ,  x y^2 /(x^2 + y^2)^2 , 0 )    for (x,y) \neq  (0,0) and z = 0  
G_2(y,z) = ( 0 , -y^2z /(y^2 + z^2)^2 ,  y z^2 /(y^2 + z^2)^2 )    for (y,z) \neq  (0,0) and x = 0  
G_3(z,x) = ( -z x^2 /(z^2 + x^2)^2 , 0 ,  x z^2 /(z^2 + x^2)^2 )    for (z,x) \neq  (0,0) and y = 0.

Observe that each field vanishes when one of its two variables is zero, so on every coordinate axis  
 (0,y,0), (x,0,0), (0,0,z)  
all three prescriptions give the common value (0,0,0); the data are therefore mutually compatible along the pairwise intersections of the planes.

Question.  Does there exist a C^1-vector field  
  F : \mathbb{R}^3 \ {(0,0,0)} \to  \mathbb{R}^3  (F = (M,N,P))  
satisfying simultaneously

(i) curl F \equiv  0 on \mathbb{R}^3 \ {(0,0,0)};  

(ii) F coincides with the given traces on the three coordinate planes, i.e.  
   F(x,y,0)=G_1(x,y), F(0,y,z)=G_2(y,z), F(x,0,z)=G_3(z,x);  

(iii) |F(r)| = O(|r|^{-1}) as |r| \to  \infty  ?

Either construct such a field or prove that none can exist.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We show that conditions (i)-(iii) are incompatible; no such vector field F exists.

1.  Topological background  
The domain \mathbb{R}^3\{0} is simply connected.  Hence any C^1-vector field whose curl is identically zero there is conservative: for every piece-wise smooth closed curve C contained in \mathbb{R}^3\{0},
  \oint _C F\cdot dr = 0.                                              (1)

2.  A closed curve touching all three planes  
Join three quarter-circles of radius 1, one in each coordinate plane, head-to-tail:

C_1 : \theta \in [0,\pi /2] \to  (cos \theta , sin \theta , 0)   (XY-plane)  
C_2 : \theta \in [0,\pi /2] \to  (0, cos \theta , sin \theta )   (YZ-plane)  
C_3 : \theta \in [0,\pi /2] \to  (sin \theta , 0, cos \theta )   (ZX-plane)

Let C = C_1 \cup  C_2 \cup  C_3.  C is a closed, piece-wise C^1 loop lying in \mathbb{R}^3\{0}; it meets each coordinate plane exactly once and avoids the coordinate axes because every component is non-negative and at least one of them is strictly positive along each segment.

Because of (1),
  \oint _C F\cdot dr = 0.                                              (2)

On each quarter-arc the integrand equals the prescribed trace (condition (ii)); we can therefore compute the three contributions explicitly.

3.  Integral over C_1 (XY-plane)  
Parameterisation: r(\theta ) = (cos \theta , sin \theta , 0), r'(\theta ) = (-sin \theta , cos \theta , 0).

G_1(r(\theta )) = ( -cos^2\theta  sin \theta  , cos \theta  sin^2\theta  , 0 )          (since (cos^2\theta +sin^2\theta )^2=1).

Dot product:  
G_1\cdot r' = (-cos^2\theta  sin \theta )(-sin \theta ) + (cos \theta  sin^2\theta )(cos \theta )  
    = 2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta .

Hence  
I_1 := \int _{C_1} F\cdot dr = \int _0^{\pi /2} 2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta  d\theta   
   = \int _0^{\pi /2} (1/2) sin^2(2\theta ) d\theta   
   = (1/4)\int _0^{\pi } sin^2u du  (u = 2\theta )  
   = (1/4)\cdot (\pi /2) = \pi /8.                               (3)

4.  Integral over C_2 (YZ-plane)  
Parameterisation: r(\theta ) = (0, cos \theta , sin \theta ), r'(\theta ) = (0, -sin \theta , cos \theta ).

G_2(r(\theta )) = ( 0, -cos^2\theta  sin \theta  , cos \theta  sin^2\theta  ).

Dot product: G_2\cdot r' = (-cos^2\theta  sin \theta )(-sin \theta ) + (cos \theta  sin^2\theta )(cos \theta )  
       = 2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta .

Therefore  
I_2 := \int _{C_2} F\cdot dr = \pi /8.                               (4)

5.  Integral over C_3 (ZX-plane)  
Parameterisation: r(\theta ) = (sin \theta , 0, cos \theta ), r'(\theta ) = (cos \theta , 0, -sin \theta ).

G_3(r(\theta )) = ( -cos \theta  sin^2\theta  , 0 , sin \theta  cos^2\theta  ).

Dot product: G_3\cdot r' = (-cos \theta  sin^2\theta )(cos \theta ) + (sin \theta  cos^2\theta )(-sin \theta )  
       = -2 cos^2\theta  sin^2\theta .

Thus  
I_3 := \int _{C_3} F\cdot dr = -\pi /8.                              (5)

6.  Total circulation around C  
Because F coincides with the three traces on the corresponding segments,

\oint _C F\cdot dr = I_1 + I_2 + I_3  
     = \pi /8 + \pi /8 - \pi /8 = \pi /8.                       (6)

7.  Contradiction  
Equations (2) and (6) are incompatible: (2) asserts the circulation is 0, whereas (6) shows it equals \pi /8.  This contradiction proves that a C^1 vector field satisfying (i)-(iii) cannot exist.

8.  Remarks  
*  Compatibility of the data along the coordinate axes (all three traces are identically zero there) removes the elementary ``two different values at one point'' obstruction, forcing one to use a genuine Stokes/circulation argument.  
*  The decay assumption (iii) is satisfied by each trace (|G_i(r)| \lesssim  |r|^{-1}) and rules out pathological compensating fields at infinity; it does not alter the contradiction obtained above.  
*  The loop C spans a surface that avoids the origin, so Stokes' theorem may legitimately be applied in both directions of the argument.

Hence no C^1-vector field F with properties (i)-(iii) exists.

• Deeper theory: understanding why a single path integral must split into pieces but still cancel necessitates a clear grasp of Stokes’ theorem on a piecewise-smooth surface, homology of punctured space, and how line-integral invariants behave under piecewise definitions.  
• Logical subtlety: none of the three planar data sets alone blocks the existence of F (each can be realised by a standard Aharonov–Bohm potential); the impossibility emerges only when all three are imposed simultaneously, so the solver must design a path that samples every restriction at once.

All of this represents a significant escalation in technical detail, length of argument, and conceptual depth compared with the original single-plane variant.
